questions.question: Explain the concept of protocol layering and state any three advantages of the layered approach in network design.
keyPoints: Protocol layering organizes network communication into distinct layers with specific responsibilities
Advantage 1: Modular design - each layer can be developed independently
Advantage 2: Interoperability - different systems can communicate
Advantage 3: Easy troubleshooting - problems can be isolated to specific layers
Advantage 4: Flexibility - layers can be modified without affecting others

questions.question: List all seven layers of the OSI model and briefly mention the main function of any three consecutive layers.
keyPoints: Seven layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Physical: Transmits raw bits over physical medium
Data Link: Provides node-to-node transfer, error detection, MAC addressing
Network: Handles routing using logical (IP) addresses
Transport: Provides end-to-end communication, segmentation, flow control

questions.question: Differentiate between the OSI model and TCP/IP model with respect to the number of layers and their corresponding layers.
keyPoints: OSI has 7 layers; TCP/IP has 4 layers
TCP/IP Network Access = OSI Physical + Data Link
TCP/IP Internet = OSI Network
TCP/IP Transport = OSI Transport
TCP/IP Application = OSI Session + Presentation + Application

questions.question: Explain the process of encapsulation and decapsulation in protocol layering with an example.
keyPoints: Encapsulation: Adding headers as data moves down through layers
Example: Application data → Transport adds segment header → Network adds packet header → Data Link adds frame header → Physical converts to bits
Decapsulation: Removing headers as data moves up through layers
Each layer reads its header and passes data to the layer above

questions.question: What is a Protocol Data Unit (PDU)? Name the PDUs at each of the five OSI layers from bottom to top.
keyPoints: PDU is the formatted unit of data at each layer
From bottom to top: Bits (Physical), Frames (Data Link), Packets (Network), Segments (Transport), Data (Application)
Alternative naming: Data at Application, Segment at Transport, Packet at Network, Frame at Data Link, Bits at Physical

questions.question: Distinguish between logical (IP) addressing and physical (MAC) addressing.
keyPoints: MAC address is physical address used at Data Link layer (Layer 2)
IP address is logical address used at Network layer (Layer 3)
MAC addresses are flat, 48-bit, assigned by manufacturer
IP addresses are hierarchical, 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6), configurable
MAC used for local communication; IP used for routing across networks

questions.question: Explain the difference between TCP and UDP protocols at the Transport layer.
keyPoints: TCP provides reliable, connection-oriented service
UDP provides unreliable, connectionless service
TCP uses acknowledgments and retransmission for reliability
TCP provides flow control and error recovery
UDP has less overhead, faster transmission
TCP suitable for applications requiring reliability (HTTP, FTP, email)
UDP suitable for real-time applications (video streaming, DNS)

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the OSI reference model architecture and discuss the functions of each of the seven layers.
keyPoints: Diagram showing 7 layers in order with arrows indicating data flow
Physical (Layer 1): Transmits raw bits, hardware (cables, hubs, repeaters)
Data Link (Layer 2): Framing, error detection, MAC addressing, switches, bridges
Network (Layer 3): Routing, logical addressing (IP), routers
Transport (Layer 4): End-to-end communication, TCP/UDP, segmentation
Session (Layer 5): Session management, synchronization, checkpoints
Presentation (Layer 6): Data translation, compression, encryption (SSL, JPEG, ASCII)
Application (Layer 7): User interface, network services (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS)

questions.question: Explain the encapsulation process in the TCP/IP model when a user sends data over a network. Show how data is transformed at each layer.
keyPoints: Start with Application layer data
Transport layer: Adds TCP/UDP header, creates segment (includes source/destination ports)
Internet layer: Adds IP header, creates packet (includes source/destination IP)
Network Access layer: Adds frame header/trailer, creates frame (includes source/destination MAC)
Physical layer: Converts to electrical signals/bits for transmission
At receiver: Reverse process (decapsulation) removing headers at each layer
Key concept: Each layer communicates only with adjacent layers

questions.question: Compare the OSI model and TCP/IP model. Explain how they differ in terms of layers and discuss why the OSI model is used as a reference model despite TCP/IP being the actual implementation.
keyPoints: OSI has 7 layers, TCP/IP has 4 layers
OSI is a theoretical/reference model; TCP/IP is practical implementation
OSI clearly separates Session and Presentation; TCP/IP combines them into Application
OSI provides more detailed, granular approach
TCP/IP was developed first for ARPANET, then standardized
OSI is used as reference for teaching, debugging, and standardizing
TCP/IP is the actual protocol suite used on the Internet
OSI provides clear boundaries for understanding; TCP/IP shows real-world implementation

questions.question: Describe the functions of the Network layer and Transport layer in the OSI model. Also explain how routers and switches operate at different layers.
keyPoints: Network layer functions: Routing, logical addressing (IP), path determination, fragmentation
Transport layer functions: End-to-end communication, segmentation, flow control, error recovery
Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network) using IP addresses for routing between networks
Switches operate at Layer 2 (Data Link) using MAC addresses for forwarding within network
Hubs and repeaters operate at Layer 1 (Physical) - no addressing used
Key difference: Routers connect different networks; switches connect devices within same network
